text: The video shows the right side of the patient with visible indirect and direct hernias, causing a noticeable bulging in the area.
The surgeon takes down a thin flap in the supravesicular space bilaterally, preparing to overlap and suture the mesh together at the symphysis pubis.
The surgeon carefully preserves some fat in the suprapubic plane during the procedure.

The surgeon carefully manipulates a thin lateral flap, ensuring protection of the lateral nerves. The colon is visually confirmed to be out of the way, despite being initially adhered with some adhesions.
The surgeon dissects the medial and lateral leaflets, ensuring to stay within the pre-peritoneal plane. The tissue appears relatively thin with minimal fat visible at the top.
The surgeon observes the hernia sac protruding into the deep internal ring, with visible edges of the sac clearly defined. The colon is confirmed to be safely positioned out of the surgical field.
The surgeon gently pulls on the pre-vesicular fat while pushing down the hernia sac, aiming to preserve the surrounding fat and tissue.
The surgeon exposes the iliac artery, maneuvering back and forth to maintain visibility and access within the surgical field.
The surgeon observes the indirect hernia sac adhered to the iliopubic tract, noting its unexpected extension further than anticipated.
The surgeon points to the fat overlaying the iliac region while working closely with the bifurcated vein. The surgical field includes a check for cord lipoma.
The surgeon performs fixation high lateral on the left side, staying above the myopectineal orifice to avoid the nerves running within that area.

The surgeon fixates the mesh at the symphysis pubis using a central stitch, aligning both meshes together. The bilateral flap closure begins with suturing the medial umbilical ligament tissue, followed by aligning the fat plane. The first stitch is placed to the left and the second to the right, using four sutures: two 11 cm ovicryls and two 9-inch ov locks to close the flaps.
